questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the typical duration of an emotion's peak intensity?

options.text: 10-15 minutes
60-90 seconds
Several hours
At least 30 minutes

questions.explanation: The peak of any intense emotion typically lasts only 60-90 seconds. If you can ride through that peak without adding fuel by fighting, suppressing, or catastrophizing, the emotion will naturally begin to recede on its own.

questions.question: What is the first step in the 'surfing your emotions' technique?

options.text: Immediately try to change the emotion to something positive
Notice and name the emotion without judgment
Distract yourself with a fun activity
Tell someone else how you are feeling

questions.explanation: The first step is to notice and name the emotion without judgment -- simply saying 'This is anxiety' or 'I am feeling a wave of anger.' Research shows that the simple act of labeling an emotion reduces its intensity in the brain.

questions.question: Why does fighting or suppressing an emotion often make it stronger?

options.text: Because emotions get stronger the more attention you pay to them
Because fighting adds shame and frustration on top of the original emotion, like holding a beach ball underwater
Because your body runs out of chemicals to process the emotion
Because it causes your heart rate to drop too quickly

questions.explanation: Fighting an emotion adds secondary emotions (shame, frustration, self-criticism) on top of the original one, making the experience more intense. The lesson compares it to holding a beach ball underwater -- the harder you push, the more forcefully it pops back up.

questions.question: What does 'Let Go of the Story' mean in the context of Riding the Wave?

options.text: Forget the event that caused the emotion
Detach from the thoughts fueling the emotion and focus on physical sensations in your body instead
Tell yourself the situation does not matter
Write down the story so you can process it later

questions.explanation: Letting go of the story means detaching from the narrative thoughts that fuel the emotion -- the 'why' and 'what if' loop -- and redirecting your attention to the physical sensations in your body. Your only job in that moment is to observe what you feel, not analyze why.

questions.question: Is Riding the Wave the same as ignoring your feelings?

options.text: Yes, you are learning to push feelings aside and move on
No, it is the opposite -- you are paying close, respectful attention to the emotion while choosing not to react impulsively
Yes, the goal is to pretend the emotion is not there
No, but you should only use it for positive emotions

questions.explanation: Riding the Wave is the opposite of ignoring. You are paying close, respectful attention to the emotion and allowing it to exist. You are simply choosing not to react impulsively -- letting the emotion speak without letting it drive your actions.

title: Riding the Wave of Emotion - Quiz
